title: Command
description: Claude Code's -p mode provides a useful example. At first glance, this may appear to be simply another way to interact with the tool. In practice, it transforms the coding agent into something much more powerful. A command such as:
content: claude -p "Implement the authentication changes described in story-123.md"

title: Repository might provide scripts
description: A repository might provide scripts such as:
content:
build.sh
test.sh
deploy.sh

title: Workflow definition
description: A simple workflow definition might look like this:
content:
story_workflow:
  stages:
    - implementation
    - testing
    - verification

title: Workflow definition
description: A workflow definition looks something like this:
content:
story_workflow:
  stages:
    - planning
    - implementation
    - testing
    - verification
    - documentation
    - pull_request

  retry_routes:
    testing_failure:
      reroute_to: testing
    verification_failure:
      reroute_to: implementation

  artifact_routes:
    implementation:
      inputs:
        - story-plan.yaml
      outputs:
        - implementation.patch
    testing:
      inputs:
        - implementation.patch
      outputs:
        - test-results.json

  escalation_rules:
    max_retries_exceeded:
      action: escalate
    ambiguous_requirements:
      action: escalate

title: Workflow state record
description: A workflow state artifact may look something like this:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "status": "running",
  "current_stage": "verification",
  "retry_count": 2,
  "branch": "story-184-auth-refactor"
}

title: Verifier result
description: A verifier artifact may resemble the following:

title: Simplified orchestration loop
description: A simplified orchestration loop may look like this:
content:
LOOP while the workflow is still active
    Determine the current workflow stage

    Execute the agent or process for that stage

    Save all generated artifacts and update workflow state

    IF verification failed THEN
        IF the workflow can retry THEN
            return execution to the appropriate retry stage
        ELSE
            escalate the workflow for human review
        END IF
    ELSE
        Advance the workflow to the next stage
    END IF
END LOOP

title: Add JWT authentication to the API.
description: For example, the interaction may begin with a request such as:
content: Add JWT authentication to the API.

title: Story definition
description: Once the developer approves the story, the planner generates the execution artifacts the harness will use---normally a story definition, which may look something like this:
content:
story:
  title: JWT Authentication

tasks:
  - implement login endpoint
  - create token middleware
  - add expiration handling
  - generate authentication tests

acceptance_criteria:
  - expired tokens return 401
  - valid tokens authenticate correctly
  - middleware remains isolated from billing logic

title: Workflow state record
description: At that point, execution begins and the harness records the current workflow state.

title: Test results
description: The story then transitions into testing. The tester agent generates and executes tests against the modified implementation and records the results in a structured form that downstream stages can evaluate.

title: Verifier result
description: In this example, the verifier determines that expired tokens are returning HTTP 500 instead of 401.

title: Retry guidance
description: Rather than restarting the workflow entirely, the harness preserves successful artifacts and retries only the unresolved failure. The retry guidance may resemble the following:
content:
retry_requirements:
  - handle token expiration explicitly
  - preserve middleware structure
  - add expiration test coverage

title: Prompt example
description: Finally, the harness generates the pull-request artifacts required to return the work to the developer in a reviewable form, as follows:
content:
pull_request/
  pr-description.md
  execution-summary.md
  verification-result.json

title: Planner prompt
description: A planner prompt may contain instructions such as:
content:
Break the requested story into implementation stages.
Preserve existing repository conventions.
Identify verification requirements.
Do not modify unrelated subsystems.

title: Prompt example
description: An implementer prompt may define operational boundaries rather than feature behavior:
content:
Implement only the current workflow stage.
Do not rewrite surrounding architecture.
Run local tests before completing the stage.
Record all modified files in the workflow artifacts.

title: Verifier prompt
description: The verifier prompt may contain instructions like this:
content:
Evaluate whether acceptance criteria were satisfied.
Reject incomplete implementations.
Identify architectural violations.
Return structured pass/fail output.

title: Execution rules
description: For example:

title: Story coordinator inside the orchestration layer
description: At a high level, a story coordinator inside the orchestration layer may look something like this:
content:
LOAD workflow definition
LOAD workflow state

CREATE git worktree
CHECKOUT workflow branch

WHILE workflow is not complete
    SELECT next stage

    LOAD stage prompt
    LOAD workflow artifacts
    LOAD prior stage outputs

    ASSEMBLE execution context
    INVOKE stage agent

    SAVE artifacts
    UPDATE workflow state

    IF result failed
        HANDLE retry or failure policy
    ELSE
        ADVANCE workflow
    END IF
END WHILE

title: Pseudocode example
description: For example, a workflow execution cycle may look something like this:
content:
START implementer subprocess
WAIT for implementation output

RUN repository tests
COLLECT test results

START verifier subprocess
WAIT for verifier result

title: .harness/context/verifier-context.json
description: During execution, orchestration assembles the runtime context for the verifier stage and injects it into the verifier prompt. The assembled context can also be persisted for inspection.
content: .harness/context/verifier-context.json

title: Assembled verifier context
description: The assembled verifier context may resemble the following.
content:
{
  "workflow_id": "story-workflow",
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "stage": "verifier",
  "available_artifacts": [
    ".harness/runs/story-184/changed-files.json",
    ".harness/runs/story-184/test-results.json",
    ".harness/runs/story-184/implementation-summary.md"
  ],
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    "duplicate webhook deliveries do not create duplicate customer charges",
    "payment processing remains idempotent during retries"
  ]
}

title: Improve duplicate payment handling
description: Consider what happens without that structure. Suppose a planner produces only:
content: Improve duplicate payment handling

title: Story definition
description: A stronger planner constrains the execution boundaries explicitly. For example, a planner may receive a request to prevent duplicate customer charges during repeated payment-webhook delivery and turn it into a bounded set of tasks with explicit constraints and expected artifacts like this:
content:
story:
  id: story-184
  title: Prevent Duplicate Charges During Webhook Retries

  description: |
    Stripe may redeliver webhook events multiple times during
    transient failures or network retries. The billing subsystem
    must process repeated delivery safely without creating
    duplicate customer charges or inconsistent payment state.

    This story adds retry-safe payment processing while
    preserving existing billing and checkout behavior.

tasks:
  - prevent_duplicate_charge_processing
  - preserve_payment_idempotency
  - validate_webhook_retry_behavior
  - verify_charge_state_consistency

scope:
  modify:
    - src/billing/payment_processor.ts
    - src/billing/payment_webhook_handler.ts
    - tests/billing/payment_retry_handling.test.ts

  do_not_modify:
    - infrastructure/
    - deployment/
    - analytics/

acceptance_criteria:
  - duplicate webhook deliveries do not create duplicate customer charges
  - payment processing remains idempotent during webhook retries
  - repeated payment events preserve consistent charge state

title: Simplified implementer context
description: A simplified implementer context may look like this:
content:
{
  "workflow_id": "story-workflow",
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "stage": "implementer",
  "objective": "Prevent duplicate payment processing during repeated Stripe webhook delivery",
  "constraints": [
    "preserve existing payment-processing workflows",
    "avoid unrelated checkout refactors",
    "modify only approved files"
  ],
  "available_artifacts": [
    "story-plan.yaml",
    "repository-summary.md",
    "payment-webhook-examples.json"
  ]
}

title: Tester prompt
description: A tester prompt may look like this.
content:
The current run's context is injected below.
From the injected changed-files record, load the implementer's source for the current run and identify which files need validation.
Generate and execute validation for the active workflow stage.
Preserve structured failure evidence.
Validate behavioral requirements independently from implementation.
Record runtime failures precisely.
Generate machine-readable outputs.

title: Test results
description: The tester then produces evidence artifacts that the verifier can consume directly. A structured test-results artifact may look like this.

title: Verifier prompt
description: A verifier prompt may contain instructions like these.
content:
Evaluate whether workflow acceptance criteria were satisfied.
Reject incomplete execution.
Identify architectural violations.
Return structured pass/fail output.
Recommend retries only when failures are bounded.

title: Verifier result
description: A verifier result artifact may look something like this.

title: Configuration example
description: That tells the workflow almost nothing useful. The implementer cannot tell what failed, what evidence supports the finding, or what scope is appropriate for the next retry. Stronger verifier output identifies the specific issue, locates it in the repository, and describes the required behavior:

title: Retry-routing artifact
description: A retry-routing artifact may look like this.

title: Prompt example
description: The difference becomes clearer when comparing planner prompts and verifier prompts directly. A planner prompt should shape decomposition behavior rather than implementation behavior.
content:
You are a story planner.

Your responsibilities are to:
- decompose work into execution stages,
- identify dependencies,
- define artifact boundaries,
- preserve workflow isolation,
- minimize retry coupling, and
- produce implementation sequencing.

Do not:
- implement code,
- speculate about implementation details,
- approve correctness, or
- rewrite requirements.

title: Prompt example
description: Verifier prompts require a different structure because verifiers directly influence retry behavior.
content:
You are a verification agent.

Your responsibilities are to:
- evaluate implementation behavior,
- identify contradictions,
- detect incomplete execution,
- validate acceptance criteria, and
- produce evidence-backed findings.

Do not:
- rewrite requirements,
- speculate without evidence,
- approve behavior you cannot verify directly, or
- recommend architectural redesign unless correctness cannot be restored within existing workflow boundaries.

Every failure claim must reference observable evidence.

title: Prompt example
description: Implementer prompts require a different form of containment. An unconstrained implementer frequently behaves like a planner during retries by redesigning surrounding logic while attempting to fix localized failures.
content:
You are an implementation agent.

Modify only artifacts explicitly referenced in the task description.

Do not:
- refactor unrelated modules,
- redesign workflow architecture,
- alter accepted interfaces unless required for correctness, or
- modify accepted artifacts outside the retry scope.

Preserve all previously accepted behavior.

title: Common layering structure looks
description: A common layering structure looks like this:
content:
[Harness Layer]
Defines durable behavioral rules shared across workflows.

[Role Layer]
Defines planner, implementer, or verifier authority.

[Workflow Layer]
Defines workflow-specific execution rules.

[Stage Layer]
Defines the objective of the current execution stage.

[Runtime State Layer]
Where the coordinator injects the current workflow state.

title: Prompt example
description: The following example illustrates how instruction layering can be applied to a verifier prompt. The example is intentionally abbreviated so the layering structure remains visible. A production verifier prompt would normally contain significantly more detail, including additional validation rules, workflow-specific constraints, artifact contracts, output requirements, and execution guidance. The purpose here is not to define a complete verifier implementation, but to demonstrate how durable behavioral instructions can be separated from evolving workflow state. Notice that the runtime state layer is a set of placeholders the coordinator fills with workflow state at runtime, rather than paths the agent must read. The verifier behavior remains stable while the injected state changes from one execution to the next.
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing structured workflows.

[Harness Layer]

All verification claims must:
- reference observable evidence,
- distinguish between confirmed failures and uncertainty, and
- avoid speculative reasoning.

[Role Layer]
You are a verification agent.

Your responsibilities are to:
- evaluate implementation behavior,
- validate acceptance criteria, and
- identify incomplete execution.

Produce verification-result.json and retry-guidance.json, each
conforming to the schema injected below.

verification-result.json schema:
{{verification_result_schema}}

retry-guidance.json schema:
{{retry_guidance_schema}}

Do not:
- rewrite requirements,
- redesign workflow architecture, or
- approve behavior you cannot verify directly.

[Workflow Layer]
This workflow prioritizes:
- artifact immutability,
- interface preservation, and
- bounded retries.

[Stage Layer]
Evaluate whether the current implementation satisfies the active acceptance criteria while preserving accepted workflow behavior.

If retry state is active:
- remain within the authorized retry scope,
- preserve accepted artifacts, and
- evaluate whether the targeted verifier findings were resolved.

[Runtime State Layer]
The coordinator injects the current workflow state into the
placeholders below. Treat the injected content as authoritative. Do
not infer workflow state from historical discussions, implementation
comments, or archived artifacts.

Acceptance criteria:
{{acceptance_criteria}}

Most recent verifier finding:
{{latest_verifier_finding}}

Accepted artifacts:
{{accepted_artifacts}}

Execution evidence:
{{execution_evidence}}

Retry state:
{{retry_state}}

title: Configuration example
description: Rendered for a first verification attempt, that runtime state layer becomes the following. The required fields carry injected content, and the optional verifier-finding and retry-state fields, which do not exist yet on a first attempt, render as None.
content:
[Runtime State Layer]
The coordinator injects the current workflow state into the
placeholders below. Treat the injected content as authoritative. Do
not infer workflow state from historical discussions, implementation
comments, or archived artifacts.

Acceptance criteria:
- duplicate webhook deliveries do not create duplicate charges
- payment processing remains idempotent during webhook retries

Most recent verifier finding:
None

Accepted artifacts:
- payment_processor.ts
- payment_webhook_handler.ts

Execution evidence:
- tests: 40 of 42 passing
- failing: duplicate_webhook_delivery_creates_two_charges

Retry state:
None

title: Prompt example
description: Consider a verifier prompt that simply asks for an opinion.
content: Review the implementation and determine whether it looks correct.

title: Prompt example
description: A stronger verifier prompt defines how correctness should be evaluated, which artifacts should be treated as authoritative, and which structured outputs the verifier must produce.
content:
Evaluate implementation behavior against the acceptance criteria
below.

Requirements:
- every failure claim must reference observable evidence,
- distinguish between confirmed failures and uncertainty,
- identify missing evidence explicitly,
- reject speculative reasoning.

Produce verification-result.json and retry-guidance.json, each
conforming to the schema injected below.

Acceptance criteria:
{{acceptance_criteria}}

Test results:
{{test_results}}

Runtime logs:
{{runtime_logs}}

Implementation summary:
{{implementation_summary}}

verification-result.json schema:
{{verification_result_schema}}

retry-guidance.json schema:
{{retry_guidance_schema}}

title: Stronger verifier distinguishes uncertainty from failure
description: A stronger verifier distinguishes uncertainty from failure.
content:
Concurrency behavior could not be verified because:
- no concurrent execution evidence was provided, and
- no concurrency tests were executed.

title: Verifier result
description: A stronger verifier produces structured artifacts that later workflow stages can consume directly.

title: Fix the verifier failures.
description: Consider the difference between an unconstrained retry instruction and a constrained retry process. An unconstrained retry instruction might say:
content: Fix the verifier failures.

title: Configuration example
description: Now compare that with a constrained retry prompt that keeps its behavioral rules fixed while the coordinator injects the current retry state:
content:
Use the injected retry state below to determine:
- authorized modification scope,
- active retry objectives, and
- protected workflow artifacts.

Do not:
- redesign workflow orchestration,
- refactor unrelated modules,
- alter accepted verifier schemas, or
- modify artifacts outside the authorized retry scope.

Preserve all accepted workflow behavior.

Verification result:
{{verification_result}}

Retry guidance:
{{retry_guidance}}

Retry state:
{{retry_state}}

Accepted artifacts:
{{accepted_artifacts}}

title: Coordinator injects that subset and nothing else
description: For example, a workflow stage executing as part of a retry may need only a small subset of the available workflow state. The coordinator injects that subset and nothing else.
content:
Injected for this retry:
- verification result
- accepted artifacts
- test results
- implementation diff
- retry summary

title: Configuration example
description: Now compare that with a structured workflow summary.
content:
Previous retries attempted:
- parser normalization changes,
- retry-state serialization fixes, and
- malformed payload recovery handling.

Confirmed failures:
- retry-state corruption during rollback recovery
- execution reporting contract violations

Accepted stable artifacts:
- verifier schema v3
- workflow execution reporting format
- accepted interface definitions

Do not revisit:
- rejected retry approaches in:
  - .harness/archive/rejected-retries/

title: Story definition
description: Consider a story that adds authenticated subscription cancellation to an existing billing application.
content:
story:
  title: Add Subscription Cancellation Support

  description:
    Add support for authenticated subscription cancellation
    while preserving billing history and existing webhook
    behavior. Existing payment-processing flows should remain
    unchanged, and the implementation should remain isolated
    from unrelated subsystems.

  tasks:
    - implement cancellation endpoint
    - update subscription state model
    - preserve billing history on cancellation
    - generate cancellation tests

  acceptance_criteria:
    - authenticated users can cancel active subscriptions
    - cancellation endpoint returns HTTP 200 on success
    - cancelled subscriptions no longer appear as active

title: Workflow state record
description: A level-three harness, therefore, needs a place to store the evolving state of the story. A simplified workflow state record might resemble:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "story_title": "Add Subscription Cancellation Support",
  "status": "running",
  "current_stage": "implementation",
  "retry_count": 0
}

title: Story definition
description: For example:
content:
story:
  id: story-184
  title: Add Subscription Cancellation Support

description:
  Add support for authenticated subscription cancellation
  while preserving billing history and existing webhook
  behavior.

acceptance_criteria:
  - Authenticated users can cancel active subscriptions
  - Cancellation endpoint returns HTTP 200 on success
  - Subscription state updates immediately after cancellation
  - Existing invoices and billing history remain accessible
  - Existing webhook processing behavior remains unchanged
  - All existing billing tests continue to pass
  - New integration tests verify cancellation behavior

technical_plan:
  domain_behavior:
    - Preserve historical billing records after cancellation
    - Prevent cancellation flow from modifying invoice history
    - Maintain compatibility with existing subscription webhooks

  implementation_steps:
    - Add authenticated cancellation endpoint
    - Update BillingService to support cancellation state transitions
    - Persist updated subscription state after cancellation
    - Add integration tests for cancellation flow
    - Verify existing webhook handlers continue functioning unchanged

likely_file_changes:
  - file: subscription_controller.ts
    reason: Add cancellation endpoint

  - file: billing_service.ts
    reason: Add cancellation state handling

  - file: subscription_repository.ts
    reason: Persist updated subscription state

  - file: subscription_integration_tests.ts
    reason: Add cancellation integration tests

verification_requirements:
  - Verify cancelled subscriptions no longer appear as active
  - Verify invoice history remains accessible after cancellation
  - Verify webhook processing behavior remains unchanged
  - Run all existing billing integration tests

constraints:
  - Do not modify webhook event schemas
  - Do not remove existing billing records
  - Preserve existing API response structure

title: Workflow state record
description: A workflow state record may look something like this:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "current_stage": "verification",
  "retry_count": 2,
  "status": "running",
  "branch": "story-184-cancellation"
}

title: Workflow execution often resembles the following
description: At a high level, workflow execution often resembles the following:
content:
LOAD workflow state

WHILE workflow is active
  DETERMINE current stage
  LOAD required artifacts
  EXECUTE stage

  SAVE generated artifacts
  UPDATE workflow state

  IF stage failed THEN
    IF retry allowed THEN
      LOOK UP workflow route
      EXECUTE workflow route
    ELSE
      ESCALATE workflow
    END IF
  ELSE
    ADVANCE workflow
  END IF
END WHILE

title: Workflow state record
description: After implementation completes, the coordinator updates the workflow state and advances execution into testing.
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "current_stage": "testing",
  "retry_count": 0,
  "status": "running",
  "branch": "story-184-cancellation",
  "artifacts": [
    "implementation.patch",
    "changed-files.json",
    "implementation-summary.md"
  ]
}

title: Test results
description: Testing then produces additional evidence that later stages can evaluate.

title: Workflow state record
description: After the retry is scheduled, the state may look something like this:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "current_stage": "implementation",
  "retry_count": 3,
  "status": "running",
  "branch": "story-184-cancellation",
  "last_verification_status": "failed",
  "artifacts": [
    "implementation.patch",
    "changed-files.json",
    "implementation-summary.md",
    "test-results.json",
    "verification-result.json"  ]
}

title: Workflow state record
description: Not every execution converges successfully. Verification may continue identifying the same unresolved defect. Retries may repeatedly fail to satisfy the acceptance criteria. Requirements may prove ambiguous enough that the harness can no longer determine the correct path forward. When that happens, the coordinator updates the workflow state again.
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "current_stage": "verification",
  "retry_count": 3,
  "status": "escalated",
  "escalation_reason": "Repeated verifier failure",
  "artifacts": [
    "implementation.patch",
    "changed-files.json",
    "implementation-summary.md",
    "test-results.json",
    "verification-result.json"
  ]
}

title: Reusable verification workflow
description: At that point, it makes sense to begin extracting reusable workflow definitions. For example, a harness may define a reusable verification workflow:
content:
verification-workflow
  requirements_verification
  security_verification
  documentation_verification

title: Reusable reporting workflow
description: It may also define a reusable reporting workflow:
content:
reporting-workflow
  execution_summary
  verification_summary
  pull_request_generation

title: Composed story workflow
description: The primary workflows can then reference these reusable definitions instead of duplicating the same execution behavior repeatedly. A story workflow may conceptually look like this:
content:
story-workflow
  implementation
  testing
  verification-workflow
  documentation
  reporting-workflow

description: A typical appeal might resemble:
content:
{
  "disputed_finding":
    "Architecture standards violation",
  "reason":
    "Experimental integration exception applies",
  "supporting_artifacts": [
    "verification-result.json",
    "architecture.md"
  ],
  "requested_action":
    "reverse"
}

title: Adjudicator decision
description: In some cases the verifier is correct.
content:
{
  "decision": "affirm",
  "reason":
    "Repository standards require the
     Strategy pattern for all payment
     providers"
}

title: Adjudicator decision
description: In other cases the verifier's conclusion is reversed.
content:
{
  "decision":  "reverse",
  "reason":
    "Repository standards explicitly permit
     alternate integration patterns for
     experimental providers"
}

title: Adjudicator decision
description: Not every dispute can be resolved from the available evidence. Some disagreements arise because requirements are ambiguous, repository standards conflict with one another, or the workflow lacks enough information to determine which interpretation is correct. In these situations the adjudicator may be unable to reach a reliable conclusion.

title: Inspection findings
description: An inspector evaluating the repository may produce findings such as:

title: Inspection findings
description: Other inspections may focus on architectural concerns that are not necessarily violations of any single standard.

title: Inspection findings
description: Still others may identify broader maintainability concerns.

title: Workflow state record
description: A workflow state record may resemble:

title: Workflow state record
description: A workflow state record may now look something like:

title: Recovery
description: At a high level, recovery may resemble the following:
content:
LOAD workflow_state

IF workflow_state.status == "escalated" THEN
    RESTORE execution artifacts
    SET current_stage = workflow_state.current_stage
ELSE IF workflow_state.status == "running" THEN
    RESTORE execution artifacts
    SET current_stage = workflow_state.current_stage
ELSE
    INITIALIZE new workflow execution
END IF

...

WHILE workflow execution is active
    ...
END WHILE

title: Why did story-184 stop?
description: Assist agents often become particularly valuable during recovery because they can help explain why execution stopped in the first place. Rather than requiring developers to manually reconstruct workflow history, the assist agent can inspect workflow state, execution history, retry history, verifier findings, appeals, and other artifacts before generating a concise explanation of the current situation. A developer may ask:
content: Why did story-184 stop?

title: Checkpoint record
description: Some harnesses extend this concept further through checkpoints. Rather than relying solely on the current workflow state, the harness periodically records known-good execution boundaries that can be used during recovery.

title: Initialization command
description: A harness may address this problem through initialization. Much like `git init` creates the foundational structure required by Git, a harness may provide an initialization mechanism that creates the foundational structure required for reliable execution. An initialization command might look like this:
content: harness init

title: Harness library
description: The simplest form of initialization is largely deterministic. A harness may contain a library of predefined documents for common technology stacks, frameworks, and architectural styles. During initialization, the appropriate templates are copied into the target repository, where they become owned by that repository. A harness library might contain assets such as these:
content:
harness-library/
  standards/
    spring-boot/
      architecture.md
      coding.md
      testing.md
    node-express/
      architecture.md
      coding.md
      testing.md

title: Guided initialization process
description: A guided initialization process might resemble:
content:
Project Type:
  REST API
Language:
  Java
Framework:
  Spring Boot
Database:
  PostgreSQL
Architecture:
  Layered

title: Parallel-execution analysis
description: An assist agent might produce an assessment such as:
content:
parallel_execution_analysis:
  safe_parallel_groups:
    - story-184
    - story-185
  defer:
    - story-186
  reason:
    story-184 and story-186 both modify
    payment-processing services

title: Environment lock
description: The worktree remains the story's private execution area. The lock file lives outside it. That way one story can claim env-1 while another story sees that env-1 is already in use and chooses a different environment or waits until one becomes available. A lock record should identify the environment, the execution, the branch, the worktree, and the time the lock was acquired so the coordinator can reason about allocation without reading story-local files. A lock file may look something like this:
content:
{
  "environment_id": "env-2",
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "branch": "story/story-184",
  "worktree": "worktrees/story-184",
  "acquired_at": "2026-08-01T14:32:00Z",
  "lease_expires_at": "2026-08-01T15:02:00Z"
}

title: Execution summary record
description: An execution summary record might resemble:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "story-184",
  "status": "escalated",
  "retry_count": 3,
  "verifier": "architecture-verifier",
  "timestamp": "2026-08-01T14:32:00Z"
}

title: Prompt example
description: Those improvements naturally become stories themselves. For example:
content:
Improve verifier reliability
Add repository-summary artifact
to implementer context
Strengthen architecture guidance
for payment-processing workflows

title: Simplified Epic Planner prompt
description: A simplified Epic Planner prompt may resemble the following:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Planner. Your responsibility
is to transform a feature request into an
executable epic. Do not begin implementation
planning until the epic has been approved by
the developer.

Before creating stories:
• identify subsystem boundaries
• identify dependencies
• identify integration concerns
• identify information that remains unknown

Ask the developer any questions required to
remove important ambiguity.

Once sufficient information is available:
• create the epic plan
• define story boundaries
• determine story order
• identify dependencies
• identify parallelization opportunities
• define epic-level objectives
• identify integration requirements

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Feature request:
{{feature_request}}

Repository standards:
{{repository_standards}}

Architecture documents:
{{architecture_documents}}

title: Epic plan
description: A simplified epic plan may look something like this:
content:
epic: Subscription Billing

summary:
  Add recurring subscription billing
  to the platform.

architecture_decisions:
  - Stripe will be used as the initial payment provider.
  - Subscription state will be stored locally.

stories:
  story-184:
    title:
      Billing Model
    objective:
      Create the subscription domain model,
      persistence structure, and lifecycle states
      required by the billing system.
    scope:
      modify:
        - billing-domain
        - subscription-persistence
      do_not_modify:
        - checkout-flow
        - reporting
    acceptance_criteria:
      - subscription records persist correctly
      - lifecycle state transitions are supported
      - existing billing behavior remains unchanged
  story-185:
    title:
      Checkout Flow
    # Additional story details omitted for brevity
  story-186:
    title:
      Webhook Processing
    # Additional story details omitted for brevity
  story-187:
    title:
      Subscription Persistence
    # Additional story details omitted for brevity
  story-188:
    title:
      Administration UI
    # Additional story details omitted for brevity
  story-189:
    title:
      Reporting
    # Additional story details omitted for brevity

dependencies:
  story-186:
    depends_on:
      - story-184
  story-187:
    depends_on:
      - story-184
  story-188:
    depends_on:
      - story-187

execution_tiers:
  tier_1:
    - story-184
    - story-185
    - story-189
  tier_2:
    - story-186
    - story-187
  tier_3:
    - story-188

epic_level_objectives:
  - validate end-to-end billing flow
  - verify reporting integration
  - coordinate production rollout

title: Simplified Epic Integrator prompt
description: A simplified Epic Integrator prompt may look like this:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Integrator. Your
responsibility is to resolve story-integration
conflicts. Do not introduce unrelated changes.

Preserve the intent of both stories, and
produce an integrated result that remains
consistent with the epic objectives.

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Epic plan:
{{epic_plan}}

Merge conflict details:
{{merge_conflict_details}}

Repository standards:
{{repository_standards}}

Architecture documents:
{{architecture_documents}}

Read the conflicting story branches directly.

title: Resulting integration artifact
description: The resulting integration artifact may look like this:
content:
{
  "status": "resolved",
  "stories": [
    "story-184",
    "story-186"
  ],
  "resolution_summary":
    "Merged webhook validation logic
     into the billing-service abstraction
     introduced by story-184"
}

title: Simplified Epic Implementer prompt
description: A simplified Epic Implementer prompt may look something like this:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Implementer. Your
responsibility is to implement epic-level
objectives that remain after story execution
has completed.

Do not duplicate work already completed by
story workflows. Do not create new tests. Do
not update architecture documentation.

Implement epic-level functionality,
configuration, and operational changes
required to complete the epic. Run existing
validation and quality checks before
completion, and preserve implementation
artifacts for later epic stages.

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Epic plan:
{{epic_plan}}

Repository standards:
{{repository_standards}}

Architecture documents:
{{architecture_documents}}

Read the integrated epic branch directly.

title: Simplified Epic Tester prompt
description: A simplified Epic Tester prompt may look like this:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Tester. Your responsibility
is to generate evidence about the completed
feature.

Do not implement feature behavior. Do not
update architecture documentation.

Create validation that exercises cross-story
behavior. Create end-to-end tests when
appropriate. Run epic-level validation, and
preserve structured evidence.

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Epic plan:
{{epic_plan}}

Epic objectives:
{{epic_objectives}}

Read the integrated epic branch directly.

title: Test results
description: As with the Story Tester, the primary output of the Epic Tester is evidence. A simplified evidence artifact may look like this:

title: Simplified Epic Verifier prompt
description: A simplified Epic Verifier prompt may look something like this:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Verifier. Your responsibility
is to determine whether the epic objectives
have been satisfied.

Evaluate the completed feature as a whole.
Reject incomplete execution. Return structured
pass/fail output, and preserve specific
findings.

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Epic plan:
{{epic_plan}}

Epic test results:
{{epic_test_results}}

Repository standards:
{{repository_standards}}

Architecture documents:
{{architecture_documents}}

Read the integrated epic branch directly.

title: Verifier result
description: A verifier result may look something like this:

title: Simplified Epic Documenter prompt
description: A simplified Epic Documenter prompt may look something like this:
content:
You are part of an agentic harness executing
structured workflows.

You are the Epic Documenter. Do not modify
implementation. Do not create tests.

Update architecture documentation. Record
integration patterns. Preserve rollout and
operational lessons. Avoid duplicating
information already captured by story
documenters.

The coordinator injects the current workflow
state into the placeholders below. Treat it
as authoritative.

Epic plan:
{{epic_plan}}

Epic verification results:
{{epic_verification_results}}

Epic implementation artifacts:
{{epic_implementation_artifacts}}

Story documentation updates:
{{story_documentation_updates}}

title: Workflow definition
description: A simplified epic workflow may look something like this:
content:
epic_workflow:
  stages:
    - epic_planning
    - story_workflow
    - integration
    - epic_implementation
    - epic_testing
    - epic_verification
    - epic_documentation

  retry_routes:
    story_workflow_failure:
      reroute_to: story_workflow
    ...

  artifact_routes:
    story_workflow:
      inputs:
        - epic-plan.yaml
        - epic-state.json
      outputs:
        - story-plan.yaml
        - implementation.patch
    ...

  escalation_rules:
    max_epic_retries_exceeded:
      action: escalate

title: Workflow state record
description: The coordinator's primary view of execution is the epic state artifact. An epic state record may look something like this:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "epic-42",
  "title": "Subscription Billing",
  "branch": "epic-42-subscription-billing",

  "status": "running",
  "current_stage": "integration",

  "stories_completed": [
    "story-184",
    "story-185",
    "story-189"
  ],

  "stories_running": [
    "story-186",
    "story-187"
  ],

  "stories_blocked": [
    "story-188"
  ],

  "retry_count": 0
}

title: run-epic epic-42
description: The developer, however, still decides which epics should execute and when they should begin. For example, a developer may choose to launch the following epics:
content:
run-epic epic-42
run-epic epic-43
run-epic epic-44

title: Simple one
description: The format of the run's specification is straightforward. A simple one might look like this:
content:
objective: |
  Add a self-service refund flow to the billing product so support
  staff can issue partial and full refunds without database access.

constraints:
  - refunds must respect the existing idempotency guarantees
  - no changes to the public checkout API
  - PCI scope must not expand

acceptance_criteria:
  - a full refund returns the charge to the customer and updates state
  - a partial refund is reflected in the invoice and the ledger
  - duplicate refund requests are rejected, not reprocessed

standards: .harness/standards/billing/
architecture: .harness/docs/BILLING_ARCHITECTURE.md

defaults:
  - refunds are soft-deleted (no developer preference; reversible)
  - refund reason codes use the existing enum (conventional)

title: Coordinator routes it to the Architect
description: A short example makes the consultation concrete. Suppose the Product Implementer is wiring two epics together and finds that the specification never said whether a failed downstream call should retry or surface the error to the caller. It raises the question. The coordinator routes it to the Architect:
content:
question: On a failed downstream billing call during refund
  processing, should the caller retry or surface the error?
architect: Surface the error. RESILIENCE.md specifies that
  write operations that may have side effects are not retried
  automatically; the caller decides. Refunds are such an
  operation.

title: Configuration example
description: A single entry, from a run that added the self-service refund flow, might look like this:
content:
question: When a partial refund is issued against an invoice
  that has multiple line items, should the refund apply to the
  whole invoice or be allocated to specific line items?
decided_by: Product Owner agent
source: no source; default applied (specification and billing
  standards are both silent on line-item allocation)
rationale: Allocating to specific line items is more flexible
  but adds a data model the spec does not call for. Whole-invoice
  refunds match the existing partial-refund behavior already in
  the billing product, so chose whole-invoice to stay consistent
  and avoid a schema change.
confidence: low
blast_radius: high (reporting and the invoice state machine
  will likely build on this refund-allocation choice)

title: Workflow state record
description: The coordinator's authoritative view of execution is product state. As with epic state, it records where the run stands now (epics completed, epics running, epics blocked, the current product stage, and overall status) and the harness preserves it alongside the product execution artifacts. A product state record may look something like this:
content:
{
  "execution_id": "product-7",
  "title": "Customer Portal",
  "branch": "product-7-customer-portal",

  "status": "running",
  "current_stage": "epic_execution",

  "epics_completed": [
    "epic-40",
    "epic-41"
  ],

  "epics_running": [
    "epic-42",
    "epic-43"
  ],

  "epics_blocked": [
    "epic-44"
  ]
}

title: Configuration example
description: It starts when a stage cannot resolve a question from its own inputs. Suppose the Product Implementer is wiring two epics together (one that records user accounts and one that exports activity reports) and finds that the specification never said what should happen to a user's exported reports when that user's account is deleted. The implementer cannot answer this from the standards or the architecture documents, because it is a question about what the product should do, not how to build it. Rather than guess or stall, the implementer emits a consultation-request artifact:
content:
question: When a user account is deleted, what happens to
  reports that user already exported---are they removed, or
  retained for the org that owns them?
kind: what

title: Product state record
description: The coordinator detects the request the same way it detects any other artifact a stage produces. It marks the epic blocked on the consultation in product state, so the run's authoritative view shows why this epic is not advancing rather than leaving it silently stalled:

title: Configuration example
description: Then it routes by kind, exactly as it routes any artifact to the stage that consumes it. A "what" question goes to the Product Owner. A "how" question goes to the Architect. This one is a "what," so the coordinator hands it to the Product Owner agent, which answers from the objective and constraints the developer approved and returns an answer artifact:
content:
answer: Retain the exported reports. They belong to the
  organization, not the individual account; the objective
  treats reports as org-owned records, and the constraints
  require deletion to remove personal data without destroying
  org history.
source: product specification (objective; data-retention
  constraint)
confidence: high

title: Start with an empty repository
description: Start with an empty repository:
content:
mkdir level-five
cd level-five
git init

title: Delegation prompt to give your assist agent
description: Here is the delegation prompt to give your assist agent:
content:
We are building a level-three agentic harness called l5: a story
execution system, implemented in Python 3 using only the standard
library. Stories enter with an approved plan, move through
implementation, testing, and verification, retry when verification
fails, and end completed or escalated. This repository is both the
harness repository and its own first target repository.

Before any code, write .harness/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md describing what we will
build. Cover the directory layout: workflows/ (workflow definitions),
prompts/ (reusable agent prompt templates with {{placeholder}}
injection), orchestration/ (the Story Coordinator and its supporting
modules), rules/ (execution rules enforced by the coordinator),
scripts/ (thin l5- entry points), templates/ (starter config and
standards that l5-init will later copy into new target
repositories), and .harness/ as target-repository state
(config.yaml, standards/, docs/, stories/, runs/, logs/).

Cover the core principles: deterministic where possible,
probabilistic where necessary; agents cooperate through structured
artifacts routed by the coordinator, never by talking to each other;
verdicts come from structured artifacts, never from agent narrative;
workflow state (state.json, what is true now) stays separate from
execution history (events.log, what happened); known context is
injected into prompt templates by the coordinator, while agents read
source code themselves by reference; rules are enforced by the
coordinator, not merely suggested to agents.

Then create README.md, .harness/config.yaml with starter settings,
starter repository standards in .harness/standards/ (architecture,
coding, testing) recording the conventions we are committing to
(scripts stay thin entry points with logic in orchestration/,
standard library only, coordinator tests never invoke a model), a
.gitignore that keeps .harness/runs/ and .harness/logs/ out of
version control, and the empty directory layout. Commit when done.

title: Code example
description: Review what the agent writes before you accept it. In the build this appendix documents, the agent produced a `.harness/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` whose core principles section reads:
content:
## Core principles

- Deterministic where possible, probabilistic where necessary. The
  Story Coordinator is deterministic Python; agents are invoked only
  where judgment is required.
- Agents cooperate through structured artifacts routed by the
  coordinator, never by talking to each other.
- Verdicts come from structured artifacts (`verification-result.json`),
  never from agent narrative.
- Workflow state (`state.json`, what is true now) is kept separate
  from execution history (`events.log`, what happened).
- Known context is injected into prompt templates by the coordinator;
  agents read source code themselves by reference because the
  coordinator cannot enumerate it in advance.
- Rules are enforced by the coordinator, not merely suggested to
  agents.

title: Prompt
description: The next delegation asks the assist agent for the harness's structural files: the target-repository configuration, the workflow definition, the execution rules, and the initialization script. Here is the prompt:
content:
Complete .harness/config.yaml with these settings: project
level-five; workflow story-workflow; branch_prefix story/;
permission_mode acceptEdits; model left unset, so runs use the
developer's default Claude Code model; stories, runs, and logs
directories
under .harness/; standards_dir .harness/standards;
architecture_docs listing .harness/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md; test_command
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q; and an allowed_tools list of
Bash permission patterns granting exactly what stage agents need:
the test command's interpreter, python3, chmod, ls, cat, and
read-only git inspection (git status, git diff, git log).

Create workflows/story-workflow.json defining four stages in order:
implementer (prompt implementer.md; outputs changed-files.json and
implementation-summary.md), tester (prompt tester.md; output
test-results.json), verifier (prompt verifier.md; output
verification-result.json; on_failure rerouting to the implementer
with max_retries 2), and documenter (prompt documenter.md; output
documentation-report.md). Add an escalation rule: exceeding max
retries escalates.

Create rules/execution-rules.json: max_retries 2,
require_verifier_pass true, and blocked_paths .git/,
.harness/runs/, and rules/.

Create scripts/l5-init: a thin Python script that initializes a
.harness/ structure (config, starter standards, and the stories,
runs, and logs directories) in any target repository, generated
from templates kept in templates/ with the project name and test
command filled in.

Commit when the four are in place.

title: Repository harness configuration
description: Start with `.harness/config.yaml`, the repository-specific settings the coordinator reads at the start of every run:
content:
# Target-repository harness configuration
project: level-five
workflow: story-workflow
branch_prefix: story/
permission_mode: acceptEdits
# model: leave unset to use the developer's default Claude Code model
stories_dir: .harness/stories
runs_dir: .harness/runs
logs_dir: .harness/logs
standards_dir: .harness/standards
architecture_docs:
  - .harness/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md
test_command: .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q
# Bash commands stage agents may run without prompting. Everything else
# is denied in headless mode, so grant exactly what the stages need.
allowed_tools:
  - "Bash(.venv/bin/python:*)"
  - "Bash(python3:*)"
  - "Bash(chmod:*)"
  - "Bash(ls:*)"
  - "Bash(cat:*)"
  - "Bash(git status:*)"
  - "Bash(git diff:*)"
  - "Bash(git log:*)"

title: Stage budgets
description: The workflow definition is `workflows/story-workflow.json`, and it is short enough to show in full:

title: Execution rules
description: The execution rules live in `rules/execution-rules.json`, also short enough to show whole:

title: Delegation prompt that starts this step
description: Here is the delegation prompt that starts this step:
content:
Write six prompt templates in prompts/, one per agent role:
planner.md, implementer.md, tester.md, verifier.md, documenter.md,
and assist.md. The four workflow-stage templates use five labeled
layers: [Harness Layer] durable rules for every agent; [Role Layer]
responsibilities and an explicit do-not list; [Workflow Layer]
priorities; [Stage Layer] the current objective; [Runtime State
Layer] {{placeholder}} fields the coordinator fills at run time,
with injected content treated as authoritative.

Role boundaries to enforce: the implementer modifies only files in
the story's approved scope, runs the existing suite locally, and
never creates, weakens, or deletes tests; the tester generates and
runs validation independently of the implementation and does not
decide whether the workflow continues; the verifier evaluates
evidence against the acceptance criteria, treats uncertainty as
distinct from failure, and never implements fixes; the documenter
updates the architecture documents and changes nothing else.

Each stage writes its output artifacts to {{run_dir}} with the JSON
schema inline in the template: changed-files.json and
implementation-summary.md from the implementer; test-results.json
from the tester; verification-result.json (status, blocking_issues,
unverified, retry_recommended) plus retry-guidance.json on a
recommended retry from the verifier; documentation-report.md from
the documenter.

planner.md drives the interactive l5-plan session: interview me,
ask about decisions that are expensive to reverse, apply stated
defaults to cheap ones, and write the approved story to
.harness/stories/story-NNN.yaml in a format you prescribe precisely
in the prompt. assist.md is the harness copilot: investigate runs
from state, events, and artifacts, cite the files used, and propose
harness-improvement stories. It does not maintain the architecture
document; the documenter stage owns that. Commit when done.

title: `prompts/verifier.md` as the assist agent wrote it
description: The most consequential of the six is the verifier, because Chapter 14 gives the verifier authority over progression: the coordinator decides whether execution advances, retries, or escalates based on what this one agent writes. Here is `prompts/verifier.md` as the assist agent wrote it:
content:
You are part of the l5 agentic harness executing structured workflows.

[Harness Layer]

All verification claims must:
- reference observable evidence,
- distinguish between confirmed failures and uncertainty, and
- avoid speculative reasoning.

[Role Layer]
You are a verification agent.

Your responsibilities are to:
- evaluate implementation behavior against the acceptance criteria,
- identify incomplete execution,
- identify violations of the repository standards, and
- produce evidence-backed findings.

Do not:
- rewrite requirements,
- implement fixes,
- speculate without evidence,
- approve behavior you cannot verify directly, or
- recommend architectural redesign unless correctness cannot be
  restored within existing workflow boundaries.

Uncertainty is not failure. If evidence is missing, say what is
missing rather than inventing a failure.

When you finish, write these files to the run directory at {{run_dir}}:

verification-result.json:
{
  "status": "passed" | "failed",
  "blocking_issues": [
    { "severity": "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "issue": "<what failed>",
      "location": "<file or area>",
      "required_behavior": "<what must be true>" }
  ],
  "unverified": ["<what could not be verified and why>"],
  "retry_recommended": true | false
}

retry-guidance.json (only when status is "failed" and a retry is
recommended):
{
  "current_focus": ["<the specific things the retry must fix>"],
  "preserve_behavior": ["<accepted behavior that must not change>"],
  "retry_scope": ["<files or areas the retry may modify>"]
}

[Workflow Layer]
This workflow prioritizes:
- verification rules that never change between retries,
- interface preservation, and
- bounded retries.

[Stage Layer]
Evaluate whether the current implementation satisfies the active
acceptance criteria while preserving accepted workflow behavior. You
may run the test suite and read the repository directly to confirm
evidence:
{{test_command}}

If retry state is active, evaluate whether the targeted verifier
findings were resolved, and confirm the retry stayed within its
authorized scope.

[Runtime State Layer]
The coordinator injects the current workflow state below. Treat the
injected content as authoritative.

Story:
{{story}}

Acceptance criteria:
{{acceptance_criteria}}

Changed files:
{{changed_files}}

Implementation summary:
{{implementation_summary}}

Test results:
{{test_results}}

Repository standards:
{{repository_standards}}

Most recent verifier finding:
{{latest_verifier_finding}}

Retry state:
{{retry_state}}

title: Configuration example
description: The other prompts follow the same five-layer shape, so excerpts suffice. The implementer's role layer is mostly a do-not list, which is Chapter 16's role-boundary pattern:
content:
Do not:
- refactor unrelated modules,
- redesign workflow architecture,
- modify accepted artifacts outside the retry scope,
- create new tests (the tester stage owns new validation), or
- weaken, skip, or delete existing tests.

title: Configuration example
description: The no-new-tests line in that list enforces the separation Chapter 14 draws between implementer and tester: evidence generated independently from implementation. The implementer runs the existing suite to catch its own regressions early, but that run is not the workflow's evidence: the tester writes and runs new validation, and the verifier evaluates what both produced. The implementer's runtime state layer shows the placeholder pattern in its simplest form:

title: Prompt
description: Now the piece that makes the harness a harness. This delegation is the largest of the build, and the prompt's length is justified: the coordinator is where the book's architecture becomes executable behavior, and its routing rules are decisions the agent cannot infer. Here is the prompt:
content:
Build the orchestration layer in orchestration/, plus entry-point
scripts and unit tests.

The Story Coordinator is deterministic: no model calls, every
decision a rule applied to a recorded fact. It loads the story
artifact from .harness/stories/ (refuse to run without one),
creates or resumes a run directory under .harness/runs/<story-id>/
(resume a run whose state.json says running; refuse to rerun one
whose status says it already ended, completed or escalated, because
the run directory holds evidence), checks out a story/<story-id>
branch, and
loops through the workflow stages. Each stage: save state.json,
append a timestamped line to events.log, assemble the stage
context, render it into the prompt template, save the rendered
prompt as prompt-<stage>-attempt-N.md, invoke the agent, then
escalate if the agent process failed or a required output artifact
is missing, and escalate if the stage's changed-files record
touches a blocked path.

Route from the verifier's artifact and nothing else: archive each
verdict as verification/iteration-N.json; passed advances; failed
with retry_recommended true and retries remaining increments the
retry count and reroutes to the stage named in the workflow's
on_failure; failed past the ceiling, or failed with
retry_recommended false, escalates immediately. Escalation writes
escalation-summary.md and exits 2. Completion writes
completion-report.md, commits the story branch, and exits 0.

Context assembly is injection: build each stage's context (story,
acceptance criteria, standards, architecture documents, blocked
paths, test command, prior stage artifacts, latest verifier
finding, retry guidance, retry state) and substitute it into the
{{placeholders}}; anything with no content renders as None. The
agent runner is the only module that talks to a model: claude -p
with the configured permission mode, model, and allowed tools,
--output-format stream-json --verbose, prompt on stdin, raw stream
appended to the story's log, final result returned. Take the
runner as a parameter so tests can inject a fake.

Scripts stay thin: l5-run <story-id> finds the target repository
by walking up to the nearest .harness/config.yaml and hands off,
erroring out with a message pointing at l5-init if no config is
found; l5-plan and l5-assist start interactive claude sessions with
their templates passed via --append-system-prompt.

Write unit tests using a fake runner that plays back scripted
stage artifacts: happy path, verification failure that retries
then passes, retries exhausted, blocked-path violation, missing
artifact, refusal to rerun an ended story, and rendering the real
templates without leftover placeholders. No test invokes a model.
Commit when done.

title: Code example
description: The coordinator is deterministic Python. Its module docstring in the real file states the contract: it never reasons, and every decision is a rule applied to a recorded fact. Chapter 17's coordinator loop---load state, then while the workflow is active, determine the stage, execute it, save artifacts, update state, and route---becomes a while-loop you can read in one pass. Here is the routing skeleton of `run_story`, condensed from the real function:

title: Code example
description: The agent runner is the only module in the harness that talks to a model, and its core is the construction of one command:
content:
cmd = [
    "claude",
    "-p",
    "--permission-mode",
    permission_mode,
    "--output-format",
    "stream-json",
    "--verbose",
]
if model:
    cmd += ["--model", model]
if allowed_tools:
    cmd += ["--allowedTools", *allowed_tools]

title: Real story request I gave it
description: Here is the real story request I gave it:
content:
Add an l5-status script that shows a snapshot of story runs. Listing
all runs should show each run's story id, status, current stage, and
retry count. Passing a story id should show that story's detail,
including its recent events.

title: Prompt example
description: This is Chapter 4's instruction drift appearing in practice, in a real planning session, on the first artifact the harness ever produced. Two corrective turns fixed it. The first named the defect and asked for a conforming rewrite:
content:
The artifact does not follow the story artifact format your
instructions prescribe... Rewrite to conform exactly, keeping the
same content.

title: Story definition
description: The approved artifact was written to `.harness/stories/story-001.yaml`, 90 lines. Here is the trimmed story:
content:
story:
  id: story-001
  title: Add l5-status script showing a snapshot of story runs
  description: |
    Add a read-only l5-status command that reports on story runs
    recorded under .harness/runs/.
    ...

tasks:
  - Create orchestration/run_status.py holding all status logic —
    discover run directories under .harness/runs/, load each run's
    state via story_coordinator.load_state, read the tail of
    events.log, and format the list and detail views as plain
    aligned text.
  - Create the thin entry point scripts/l5-status that parses the
    optional story-id argument, locates the target repository by
    walking up to the nearest .harness/config.yaml (same approach
    as l5-run), and delegates to run_status.
  ...

acceptance_criteria:
  - "scripts/l5-status with no arguments lists every directory under
    .harness/runs/, showing story id, status, current stage, and
    retry count per run, sorted by story id."
  - "scripts/l5-status <story-id> shows that run's full state fields
    and the last 10 lines of its events.log."
  ...
  - "New unit tests cover listing, detail view, event tailing, and
    the error cases using fixture run directories with no model
    invocations; the full suite passes via
    .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q."

scope:
  modify:
    - scripts/          # new l5-status entry point
    - orchestration/    # new run_status.py module
    - tests/            # new unit tests
  do_not_modify:
    - orchestration/story_coordinator.py
    - workflows/
    - prompts/
    - rules/
    ...

title: One command starts the run
description: One command starts the run:
content: scripts/l5-run story-001

title: Configuration example
description: The script walks up to the nearest `.harness/config.yaml`, hands the story to the coordinator, and the coordinator creates the `story/story-001` branch, the run directory, and starts the loop. When I ran this the first time, here is the complete event history it left behind:
content:
[2026-07-06 13:00:01] workflow started for story-001
[2026-07-06 13:00:01] implementer stage started
[2026-07-06 13:02:47] implementer stage completed
[2026-07-06 13:02:47] tester stage started
[2026-07-06 13:05:00] tester stage completed
[2026-07-06 13:05:00] verifier stage started
[2026-07-06 13:07:11] verification failed; retry 1 of 2 rerouted to implementer
[2026-07-06 13:07:11] implementer stage started
[2026-07-06 13:08:26] implementer stage completed
[2026-07-06 13:08:26] tester stage started
[2026-07-06 13:10:21] tester stage completed
[2026-07-06 13:10:21] verifier stage started
[2026-07-06 13:11:45] escalated: verification failed and the verifier did not recommend a retry

title: Verifier result
description: The run took twelve minutes, spent one retry, and ended in an escalation with exit code 2. It was not the transcript I was hoping for, but the run directory explains itself, which is the whole point of the architecture.[^assist-reads-evidence] The console showed the same lines as they happened, `state.json` recorded `status: escalated` with the retry count, and `escalation-summary.md` gave the human-facing summary with pointers into the evidence. The verifier's second verdict, archived at `verification/iteration-2.json`, is worth reading closely because it illustrates the book's verification chapter operating under real conditions:
content:
{
  "status": "failed",
  "blocking_issues": [
    {
      "severity": "high",
      "issue": "The targeted retry finding is unresolved: the test
        suite has still never been executed. This verifier
        independently attempted '.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q'
        in retry iteration 1 and it was denied by the session
        permission system ('This command requires approval'), matching
        the denials recorded by the implementer, tester, and prior
        verifier ... test-results.json records tests_run: 0. ... no
        runtime evidence exists for any acceptance criterion.",
      ...
    },
    ...
  ],
  "unverified": [
    "Runtime behavior of every acceptance criterion (listing view,
     detail view, event tailing, error-path exit codes ...). The
     prior verifier's static reading found orchestration/run_status.py,
     scripts/l5-status, tests/test_run_status.py, and
     story_coordinator.load_state mutually consistent with all
     criteria and constraints ... but none of it has been executed.",
    ...
    "... A further automated retry cannot resolve either blocking
     issue; operator action is required: run 'chmod +x
     scripts/l5-status' and '.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q'
     manually, or grant Bash execution permission for these commands,
     then re-run verification."
  ],
  "retry_recommended": false
}

description: The third run left this event history:
content:
[2026-07-06 13:27:34] workflow started for story-001

title: Test results
description: It took six minutes and needed no retries. The test evidence this time is real:
content:
{
  "status": "passed",
  "tests_written": 9,
  "tests_run": 31,
  "tests_passed": 31,
  "tests_failed": 0,
  "failures": []
}

title: Run directory ends with `completion-report.md`
description: The run directory ends with `completion-report.md`:
content:
# story-001 Completion Report

## Story
Add l5-status script showing a snapshot of story runs

## Outcome
Completed on branch story/story-001 after 0 retries.

## Evidence
- test-results.json
- verification/iteration-1.json (passed)
- documentation-report.md

## Next Step
Review the branch and merge it when you accept the story.

title: Configuration example
description: Then `l5-run story-002` executed it: one pass, no retries, 42 tests passing with 7 new ones from the tester, verification passed, architecture document updated, branch committed. The verifier's verdict carried one more honest nuance worth reading:
content:
"unverified": [
  "tester-changed-files.json is absent from this run directory, so
   the runtime injection of the tester's record could not be observed
   end-to-end in this run. This is expected: the coordinator loaded
   the workflow definition before this story modified it, so the
   tester stage ran under the pre-story-002 definition. The behavior
   itself is verified by unit tests ... and takes effect for runs
   started after this story merges."
]
